title: Score-Based Generative Modeling with Critically-Damped Langevin Diffusion

paragraphs.text:
Published as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

# SCORE-BASED GENERATIVE MODELING WITH CRITICALLY-DAMPED LANGEVIN DIFFUSION

**Tim Dockhorn[1,2,3,]** _[∗]_

**Arash Vahdat[1] Karsten Kreis[1]**

1NVIDIA 2University of Waterloo 3Vector Institute tim.dockhorn@uwaterloo.ca, _{_ avahdat,kkreis _}_ @nvidia.com

## ABSTRACT

Score-based generative models (SGMs) have demonstrated remarkable synthesis quality. SGMs rely on a diffusion process that gradually perturbs the data towards a tractable distribution, while the generative model learns to denoise. The complexity of this denoising task is, apart from the data distribution itself, uniquely determined by the diffusion process. We argue that current SGMs employ overly simplistic diffusions, leading to unnecessarily complex denoising processes, which limit generative modeling performance. Based on connections to statistical mechanics, we propose a novel _critically-damped Langevin diffusion_ (CLD) and show that CLD-based SGMs achieve superior performance. CLD can be interpreted as running a joint diffusion in an extended space, where the auxiliary variables can be considered “velocities” that are coupled to the data variables as in Hamiltonian dynamics. We derive a novel score matching objective for CLD and show that the model only needs to learn the score function of the conditional distribution of the velocity given data, an easier task than learning scores of the data directly. We also derive a new sampling scheme for efficient synthesis from CLD-based diffusion models. We find that CLD outperforms previous SGMs in synthesis quality for similar network architectures and sampling compute budgets. We show that our novel sampler for CLD significantly outperforms solvers such as Euler–Maruyama. Our framework provides new insights into score-based denoising diffusion models and can be readily used for high-resolution image synthesis. Project page and code: https://nv-tlabs.github.io/CLD-SGM.

## 1 INTRODUCTION

Score-based generative models (SGMs) and denoising diffusion probabilistic models have emerged as a promising class of generative models (Sohl-Dickstein et al., 2015; Song et al., 2021c;b; Vahdat et al., 2021; Kingma et al., 2021). SGMs offer high quality synthesis and sample diversity, do not require adversarial objectives, and have found applications in image (Ho et al., 2020; Nichol & Dhariwal, 2021; Dhariwal & Nichol, 2021; Ho et al., 2021), speech (Chen et al., 2021; Kong et al., 2021; Jeong et al., 2021), and music synthesis (Mittal et al., 2021), image editing (Meng et al., 2021; Sinha et al., 2021; Furusawa et al., 2021), super-resolution (Saharia et al., 2021; Li et al., 2021), image-to-image translation (Sasaki et al., 2021), and 3D shape generation (Luo & Hu, 2021; Zhou et al., 2021). SGMs use a diffusion process to gradually add noise to the data, transforming a complex data distribution into an analytically tractable prior distribution. A neural network is then utilized to learn the score function—the gradient of the log probability density—of the perturbed data. The learnt scores can be used to solve a stochastic differential equation (SDE) to synthesize new samples. This corresponds to an iterative denoising process, inverting the forward diffusion.

In the seminal work by Song et al. (2021c), it has been shown that the score function that needs to be learnt by the neural network is uniquely determined by the forward diffusion process. Consequently, the complexity of the learning problem depends, other than on the data itself, only on the diffusion. Hence, the diffusion process is the key component of SGMs that needs to be revisited to further improve SGMs, for example, in terms of synthesis quality or sampling speed.

> _∗_ Work done during internship at NVIDIA.

Figure 1: In critically-damped Langevin diffusion, the data **x** _t_ is augmented with a velocity **v** _t_ . A diffusion coupling **x** _t_ and **v** _t_ is run in the joint data-velocity space (probabilities in **red** ). Noise is injected only into **v** _t_ . This leads to smooth diffusion trajectories ( **green** ) for the data **x** _t_ . Denoising only requires _∇_ **v** _t_ log _p_ ( **v** _t|_ **x** _t_ ).

Inspired by statistical mechanics (Tuckerman, 2010), we propose a novel forward diffusion process, the _critically-damped Langevin diffusion (CLD)_ . In CLD, the data variable, **x** _t_ (time _t_ along the diffusion), is augmented with an additional “velocity” variable **v** _t_ and a diffusion process is run in the joint data-velocity space. Data and velocity are coupled to each other as in Hamiltonian dynamics, and noise is injected only into the velocity variable. As in Hamiltonian Monte Carlo (Duane et al., 1987; Neal, 2011), the Hamiltonian component helps to efficiently traverse the joint data-velocity space and to transform the data distribution into the prior distribution more smoothly. We derive the corresponding score matching objective and show that for CLD the neural network is tasked with learning only the score of the conditional distribution of velocity given data _∇_ **v** _t_ log _pt_ ( **v** _t|_ **x** _t_ ), which is arguably easier than learning the score of diffused data directly. Using techniques from molecular dynamics (Bussi & Parrinello, 2007; Tuckerman, 2010; Leimkuhler & Matthews, 2013), we also derive a new SDE integrator tailored to CLD’s reverse-time synthesis SDE.

We extensively validate CLD and the novel SDE solver: **(i)** We show that the neural networks learnt in CLD-based SGMs are smoother than those of previous SGMs. **(ii)** On the CIFAR-10 image modeling benchmark, we demonstrate that CLD-based models outperform previous diffusion models in synthesis quality for similar network architectures and sampling compute budgets. We attribute these positive results to the Hamiltonian component in the diffusion and to CLD’s easier score function target, the score of the velocity-data conditional distribution _∇_ **v** _t_ log _pt_ ( **v** _t|_ **x** _t_ ). **(iii)** We show that our novel sampling scheme for CLD significantly outperforms the popular Euler– Maruyama method. **(iv)** We perform ablations on various aspects of CLD and find that CLD does not have difficult-to-tune hyperparameters.

In summary, we make the following technical contributions: **(i)** We propose CLD, a novel diffusion process for SGMs. **(ii)** We derive a score matching objective for CLD, which requires only the conditional distribution of velocity given data. **(iii)** We propose a new type of denoising score matching ideally suited for scalable training of CLD-based SGMs. **(iv)** We derive a tailored SDE integrator that enables efficient sampling from CLD-based models. **(v)** Overall, we provide novel insights into SGMs and point out important new connections to statistical mechanics.

3 CRITICALLY-DAMPED LANGEVIN DIFFUSION

We propose to augment the data **x** _t ∈_ R _[d]_ with auxiliary _velocity_[1] variables **v** _t ∈_ R _[d]_ and utilize a diffusion process that is run in the joint **x** _t_ - **v** _t_ -space. With **u** _t_ = ( **x** _t,_ **v** _t_ ) _[⊤] ∈_ R[2] _[d]_ , we set

where _⊗_ denotes the Kronecker product. The coupled SDE that describes the diffusion process is

which corresponds to _Langevin dynamics_ in each dimension. That is, each _xi_ is independently coupled to a velocity _vi_ , which explains the blockwise structure of _**f**_ and _**G**_ . The _mass M ∈_ R[+] is a hyperparameter that determines the coupling between the **x** _t_ and **v** _t_ variables; _β ∈_ R[+] is a constant time rescaling chosen such that the diffusion converges to its equilibrium distribution within _t ∈_ [0 _, T_ ] (in practice, we set _T_ =1) when initialized from a data-defined non-equilibrium state and is analogous to _β_ ( _t_ ) in previous diffusions (we could also use time-dependent _β_ ( _t_ ), but found constant _β_ ’s to work well, and therefore opted for simplicity); Γ _∈_ R[+] is a _friction_ coefficient that determines the strength of the noise injection into the velocities. Notice that the SDE in Eq. (5) consists of two components. The _H_ term represents a Hamiltonian component. Hamiltonian dynamics are frequently used in Markov chain Monte Carlo methods to accelerate sampling and efficiently explore complex probability distributions (Neal, 2011). The Hamiltonian component in our diffusion process plays a similar role and helps to quickly and smoothly converge the initial joint data-velocity distribution to the equilibrium, or prior (see Fig. 1). Furthermore, Hamiltonian dynamics on their own are trivially invertible (Tuckerman, 2010), which intuitively is also beneficial in our situation when using this diffusion for training SGMs. The _O_ term corresponds to an Ornstein-Uhlenbeck process (S¨arkk¨a & Solin, 2019) in the velocity component, which injects noise such that the diffusion dynamics properly converge to equilibrium for any Γ _>_ 0. It can be shown that the equilibrium distribution of this diffusion is _p_ EQ( **u** ) = _N_ ( **x** ; **0** _d,_ _**I** d_ ) _N_ ( **v** ; **0** _d, M_ _**I** d_ ) (see App. B.2).

There is a crucial balance between _M_ and Γ (McCall, 2010): For Γ[2] _<_ 4 _M_ ( _underdamped_ Langevin dynamics) the Hamiltonian component dominates, which implies oscillatory dynamics of **x** _t_ and **v** _t_ that slow down convergence to equilibrium. For Γ[2] _>_ 4 _M_ ( _overdamped_ Langevin dynamics) the _O_ - term dominates which also slows down convergence, since the accelerating effect by the Hamiltonian component is suppressed due to the strong noise injection. For Γ[2] =4 _M_ ( _critical damping_ ), an ideal balance is achieved and convergence to _p_ EQ( **u** ) occurs as fast as possible in a smooth manner without oscillations (also see discussion in App. A.1) (McCall, 2010). Hence, we propose to set Γ[2] =4 _M_ and call the resulting diffusion _critically-damped Langevin diffusion (CLD)_ (see Fig. 1).

Diffusions such as the VPSDE (Song et al., 2021c) correspond to overdamped Langevin dynamics with high friction coefficients Γ (see App. A.2). Furthermore, in previous works noise is injected directly into the data variables (pixels, for images). In CLD, only the velocity variables are subject to direct noise and the data is perturbed only indirectly due to the coupling between **x** _t_ and **v** _t_ .

> 1We call the auxiliary variables _velocities_ , as they play a similar role as velocities in physical systems. Formally, our velocity variables would rather correspond to physical momenta, but the term momentum is already widely used in machine learning and our mass _M_ is unitless anyway.

## 3.1 SCORE MATCHING OBJECTIVE

Considering the appealing convergence properties of CLD, we propose to utilize CLD as forward diffusion process in SGMs. To this end, we initialize the joint _p_ ( **u** 0)= _p_ ( **x** 0) _p_ ( **v** 0)= _p_ data( **x** 0) _N_ ( **v** 0; **0** _d, γM_ _**I** d_ ) with hyperparameter _γ<_ 1 and let the distribution diffuse towards the tractable equilibrium—or prior—distribution _p_ EQ( **u** ). We can then learn the corresponding score functions and define CLD-based SGMs. Following a similar derivation as Song et al. (2021b), we obtain the score matching (SM) objective (see App. B.3):

Notice that this objective requires only the velocity gradient of the log-density of the joint distribution, i.e., _∇_ **v** _t_ log _pt_ ( **u** _t_ ). This is a direct consequence of injecting noise into the velocity variables _only_ . Without loss of generality, _pt_ ( **u** _t_ )= _pt_ ( **x** _t,_ **v** _t_ )= _pt_ ( **v** _t|_ **x** _t_ ) _pt_ ( **x** _t_ ). Hence,

This means that in CLD the neural network-defined score model _s_ _**θ**_ ( **u** _t, t_ ) only needs to learn the score of the conditional distribution _pt_ ( **v** _t|_ **x** _t_ ), an arguably easier task than learning the score of _pt_ ( **x** _t_ ), as in previous works, or of the joint _pt_ ( **u** _t_ ). This is the case, because our velocity distribution is initialized from a simple Normal distribution, such that _pt_ ( **v** _t|_ **x** _t_ ) is closer to a Normal distribution for all _t≥_ 0 (and for any **x** _t_ ) than _pt_ ( **x** _t_ ) itself. This is most evident at _t_ =0: The data and velocity distributions are independent at _t_ =0 and the score of _p_ 0( **v** 0 _|_ **x** 0)= _p_ 0( **v** 0) simply corresponds to the score of the Normal distribution _p_ 0( **v** 0) from which the velocities are initialized, whereas the score of the data distribution _p_ 0( **x** 0) is highly complex and can even be unbounded (Kim et al., 2021). We empirically verify the reduced complexity of the score of _pt_ ( **v** _t|_ **x** _t_ ) in Fig. 2. We find that the score that needs to be learnt by the model is more similar to a score corresponding to a Normal distribution for CLD than for the VPSDE. We also measure the complexity of the neural networks that were learnt to model this score via the squared Frobenius norm of their Jacobians. We find that the CLD-based SGMs have significantly simpler and smoother neural networks than VPSDE-based SGMs for most _t_ , in particular when leveraging a mixed score formulation (see next section).

## 3.3 SAMPLING FROM CLD-BASED SGMS

To sample from the CLD-based SGM we can either directly simulate the reverse-time diffusion process (Eq. (2)) or, alternatively, solve the corresponding probability flow ODE (Song et al., 2021c;b) (see App. B.5). To simulate the SDE of the reverse-time diffusion process, previous works often relied on Euler-Maruyama (EM) (Kloeden & Platen, 1992) and related methods (Ho et al., 2020; Song et al., 2021c; Jolicoeur-Martineau et al., 2021a). We derive a new solver, tailored to CLD-based models. Here, we provide the high-level ideas and derivations (see App. D for details).
Our generative SDE can be written as (with **u** ¯ _t_ = **u** _T −t_ , **x** ¯ _t_ = **x** _T −t_ , **v** ¯ _t_ = **v** _T −t_ ):

It consists of a Hamiltonian component _AH_ , an Ornstein-Uhlenbeck process _AO_ , and the score model term _S_ . We could use EM to integrate this SDE; however, standard Euler methods are not well-suited for Hamiltonian dynamics (Leimkuhler & Reich, 2005; Neal, 2011). Furthermore, if _S_ was 0, we could solve the SDE in closed form. This suggests the construction of a novel integrator.
We use the Fokker-Planck operator[2] formalism (Tuckerman, 2010; Leimkuhler & Matthews, 2013; 2015). Using a similar notation as Leimkuhler & Matthews (2013), the Fokker-Planck equation

A finer discretization results in a smaller error (since _N_ = _t/δt_ , the error effectively scales as _O_ ( _δt_[2] ) for fixed _t_ ). Hence, this implies an integration method. Indeed, _e δt_ 2 _L_[ˆ] _[∗] A_ ¯ **u** _t_ is available in closed form, as mentioned before; however, _e[δt] L_[ ˆ] _[∗] S_ ¯ **u** _t_ is not. Therefore, we approximate this latter component of the integrator via a standard Euler step. Thus, the integrator formally has an error of the same order as standard EM methods. Nevertheless, as long as the dynamics is not dominated by the _S_ component, our proposed integration scheme is expected to be more accurate than EM, since we split off the analytically tractable part and only use an Euler approximation for the _S_ term. Recall that the model only needs to learn the score of the conditional distribution _pt_ ( **v** _t|_ **x** _t_ ), which is close to Normal for much of the diffusion, in which case the _S_ term will indeed be small. This suggests that the generative SDE dynamics are in fact dominated by _AH_ and _AO_ in practice. Note that only the propagator _e[δt] L_[ ˆ] _[∗] S_ is computationally expensive, as it involves evaluating the neural network. We coin our novel SDE integrator for CLD-based SGMs _Symmetric Splitting CLD Sampler_ (SSCS). A detailed derivation, analyses, and a formal algorithm are presented in App. D.

## 4 RELATED WORK

**Relations to Statistical Mechanics and Molecular Dynamics.** Learning a mapping between a simple, tractable and a complex distribution as in SGMs is inspired by annealed importance sampling (Neal, 2001) and the Jarzynski equality from non-equilibrium statistical mechanics (Jarzynski, 1997a;b; 2011; Bahri et al., 2020). However, after Sohl-Dickstein et al. (2015), little attention has been given to the origins of SGMs in statistical mechanics. Intuitively, in SGMs the diffusion process is initialized in a non-equilibrium state **u** 0 and we would like to bring the system to equilibrium, i.e., the tractable prior distribution, _as quickly and as smoothly as possible_ to enable efficient denoising. This “equilibration problem” is a much-studied problem in statistical mechanics, particularly in molecular dynamics, where a molecular system is often simulated in thermodynamic equilibrium. Algorithms to quickly and smoothly bring a system to and maintain at equilibrium are known as _thermostats_ . In fact, CLD is inspired by the Langevin thermostat (Bussi & Parrinello, 2007). In molecular dynamics, advanced thermostats are required in particular for “multiscale” systems that show complex behaviors over multiple time- and length-scales. Similar challenges also arise when modeling complex data, such as natural images. Hence, the vast literature on thermostats (Andersen, 1980; Nos´e, 1984; Hoover, 1985; Martyna et al., 1992; H¨unenberger, 2005; Bussi et al., 2007; Ceriotti et al., 2009; 2010; Tuckerman, 2010) may be valuable for the development of future SGMs. Also the framework for developing SSCS is borrowed from statistical mechanics. The same techniques have been used to derive molecular dynamics algorithms (Tuckerman et al., 1992; Bussi & Parrinello, 2007; Ceriotti et al., 2010; Leimkuhler & Matthews, 2013; 2015; Kreis et al., 2017).

**Further Related Work.** Generative modeling by learning stochastic processes has a long history (Movellan, 2008; Lyu, 2009; Sohl-Dickstein et al., 2011; Bengio et al., 2014; Alain et al., 2016; Goyal et al., 2017; Bordes et al., 2017; Song & Ermon, 2019; Ho et al., 2020). We build on Song et al. (2021c), which introduced the SDE framework for modern SGMs. Nachmani et al. (2021) recently introduced non-Gaussian diffusion processes with different noise distributions. However, the noise is still injected directly into the data, and no improved sampling schemes or training objectives are introduced. Vahdat et al. (2021) proposed LSGM, which is complementary to CLD: we improve

6

Published as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

the diffusion process itself, whereas LSGM “simplifies the data” by first embedding it into a smooth latent space. LSGM is an overall more complicated framework, as it is trained in two stages and relies on additional encoder and decoder networks. Recently, techniques to accelerate sampling from pre-trained SGMs have been proposed (San-Roman et al., 2021; Watson et al., 2021; Kong & Ping, 2021; Song et al., 2021a). Importantly, these methods usually do not permit straightforward loglikelihood estimation. Furthermore, they are originally not based on the continuous time framework, which we use, and have been developed primarily for discrete-step diffusion models.

A complementary work to CLD is “Gotta Go Fast” (GGF) (Jolicoeur-Martineau et al., 2021a), which introduces an adaptive SDE solver for SGMs, tuned towards image synthesis. GGF uses standard Euler-based methods under the hood (Kloeden & Platen, 1992; Roberts, 2012), in contrast to our SSCS that is derived from first principles. Furthermore, our SDE integrator for CLD does not make any data-specific assumptions and performs extremely well even without adaptive step sizes.

Some works study SGMs for maximum likelihood training (Song et al., 2021b; Kingma et al., 2021; Huang et al., 2021). Note that we did not focus on training our models towards high likelihood. Furthermore, Chen et al. (2020) and Huang et al. (2020) recently trained augmented Normalizing Flows, which have conceptual similarities with our velocity augmentation. Methods leveraging auxiliary variables similar to our velocities are also used in statistics—such as Hamiltonian Monte Carlo (Neal, 2011)—and have found applications, for instance, in Bayesian machine learning (Chen et al., 2014; Ding et al., 2014; Shang et al., 2015). As shown in Ma et al. (2019), our velocity is equivalent to momentum in gradient descent and related methods (Polyak, 1964; Kingma & Ba, 2015). Momentum accelerates optimization; the velocity in CLD accelerates mixing in the diffusion process. Lastly, our CLD method can be considered as a second-order Langevin algorithm, but even higher-order schemes are possible (Mou et al., 2021) and could potentially further improve SGMs.

## 5 EXPERIMENTS

**Architectures.** We focus on image synthesis and implement CLD-based SGMs using NCSN++ and DDPM++ (Song et al., 2021c) with 6 input channels (for velocity and data) instead of 3.

**Relevant Hyperparameters.** CLD’s hyperparameters are chosen as _β_ =4, Γ=1 (or equivalently _M[−]_[1] =4) in all experiments. We set the variance scaling of the inital velocity distribution to _γ_ =0 _._ 04 and use the proposed HSM objective with the weighting _λ_ ( _t_ )= _ℓ[−] t_[2][, which promotes image quality.]

**Sampling.** We generate model samples via: **(i)** Probability flow using a Runge–Kutta 4(5) method; reverse-time generative SDE sampling using either **(ii)** EM or **(iii)** our SSCS. For methods without adaptive stepsize (EM and SSCS), we use evaluation times chosen according to a quadratic function, like previous work (Song et al., 2021a; Kong & Ping, 2021; Watson et al., 2021) (indicated by QS).

**Evaluation.** We measure image sample quality for CIFAR-10 via Fr´echet inception distance (FID) with 50k samples (Heusel et al., 2017). We also evaluate an upper bound on the negative loglikelihood (NLL): _−_ log _p_ ( **x** 0) _≤−_ E **v** 0 _∼p_ ( **v** 0) log _pε_ ( **x** 0 _,_ **v** 0) _−H_ , where _H_ is the entropy of _p_ ( **v** 0) and log _pε_ ( **x** 0 _,_ **v** 0) is an unbiased estimate of log _p_ ( **x** 0 _,_ **v** 0) from the probability flow ODE (Grathwohl et al., 2019; Song et al., 2021c). As in Vahdat et al. (2021), the stochasticity of log _pε_ ( **x** _,_ **v** ) prevents us from performing importance weighted NLL estimation over the velocity distribution (Burda et al., 2015). We also record the number of function—neural network—evaluations (NFEs) during synthesis when comparing sampling methods. All implementation details in App. B.5 and E.

## 5.1 IMAGE GENERATION

Following Song et al. (2021c), we focus on the widely used CIFAR-10 unconditional image generation benchmark. Our CLD-based SGM achieves an FID of 2.25 based on the probability flow ODE and an FID of 2.23 via simulating the generative SDE (Tab. 1). The only models marginally outperforming CLD are LSGM (Vahdat et al., 2021) and NSCN++/VESDE with 2,000 step predictorcorrector (PC) sampling (Song et al., 2021c). However, LSGM uses a model with _≈_ 475 _M_ parameters to achieve its high performance, while we obtain our numbers with a model of _≈_ 100 _M_ parameters. For a fairer comparison, we trained a smaller LSGM also with _≈_ 100 _M_ parameters, which is reported as “LSGM-100M” in Tab. 1 (details in App. E.2.7). Our model has a significantly better FID score than “LSGM-100M”. In contrast to NSCN++/VESDE, we achieve extremely strong results with much fewer NFEs (for example, see _n∈{_ 150 _,_ 275 _,_ 500 _}_ in Tab. 3 and also Tab. 2)—the VESDE performs poorly in this regime. We conclude that when comparing models with similar

## 5.2 SAMPLING SPEED AND SYNTHESIS QUALITY TRADE-OFFS

We analyze the sampling speed vs. synthesis quality trade-off for CLD-SGMs and study SSCS’s performance under different NFE budgets (Tabs. 2 and 3). We compare to Song et al. (2021c) and use EM to solve the generative SDE for their VPSDE and PC (reverse-diffusion + Langevin sampler) for the VESDE model. We also compare to the GGF (Jolicoeur-Martineau et al., 2021a) solver for the generative SDE as well as probability flow ODE sampling with a higher-order adaptive step size solver. Further, we compare to LSGM (Vahdat et al., 2021) (using our LSGM-100M), which also uses probability flow sampling. With one exception (VESDE with 2,000 NFE) our CLD-SGM outperforms all baselines, both for adaptive and fixed-step size methods. More results in App. F.2.

Several observations stand out: **(i)** As expected (Sec. 3.3), for CLD, SSCS significantly outperforms EM under limited NFE budgets. When using a fine discretization of the SDE (high NFE), the two perform similarly, which is also expected, as the errors of both methods will become negligible. **(ii)** In the adaptive solver setting, using a simpler ODE solver, we even outperform GGF, which is tuned towards image synthesis. **(iii)** Our CLD-SGM also outperforms the LSGM-100M model in terms of FID. It is worth noting, however, that LSGM was designed primarily for faster synthesis, which it achieves by modeling a smooth distribution in latent space instead of the more complex data distribution directly. This suggests that it would be promising to combine LSGM with CLD and train a CLD-based LSGM, combining the strengths of the two approaches. **Initial Velocity Distribution:** Tab. 5 shows results for a CLD-SGM trained with different initial velocity variance scalings _γ_ . Varying _γ_ similarly has only a small effect, but small _γ_ seems slightly beneficial for FID, while the NLL bound suffers a bit. Due to our focus on synthesis quality as measued by FID, we opted for small _γ_ . Intuitively, this means that the data at _t_ =0 is “at rest”, and noise flows from the velocity into the data variables only slowly.

**Mixed Score:** Similar to previous work (Vahdat et al., 2021), we find training with the mixed score (MS) parametrization (Sec. 3.2) beneficial. With MS, we achieve an FID of 3.14, without only 3.56.

**Hybrid Score Matching:** We also tried training with regular DSM, instead of HSM. However, training often became unstable. As discussed in Sec. 3.2, this is likely because when using standard DSM our CLD would suffer from unbounded scores close to _t_ =0, similar to previous SDEs (Kim et al., 2021). Consequently, we consider our novel HSM a crucial element for training CLD-SGMs.

We conclude that CLD does not come with difficult-to-tune hyperparameters. We expect our chosen hyperparameters to immediately translate to new tasks and models. In fact, we used the same _M[−]_[1] , _γ_ , MS and HSM settings for CIFAR-10 and CelebA-HQ-256 experiments without fine-tuning.

## 6 CONCLUSIONS

We presented _critically-damped Langevin diffusion_ , a novel diffusion process for training SGMs. CLD diffuses the data in a smoother, easier-to-denoise manner compared to previous SGMs, which results in smoother neural network-parametrized score functions, fast synthesis, and improved expressivity. Our experiments show that CLD outperforms previous SGMs on image synthesis for similar-capacity models and sampling compute budgets, while our novel SSCS is superior to EM in CLD-based SGMs. From a technical perspective, in addition to proposing CLD, we derive CLD’s score matching objective termed as HSM, a variant of denoising score matching suited for CLD, and we derive a tailored SDE integrator for CLD. Inspired by methods used in statistical mechanics, our work provides new insights into SGMs and implies promising directions for future research.

We believe that CLD can potentially serve as the backbone diffusion process of next generation SGMs. Future work includes using CLD-based SGMs for generative modeling tasks beyond images, combining CLD with techniques for accelerated sampling from SGMs, adapting CLD-based SGMs towards maximum likelihood, and utilizing other thermostating methods from statistical mechanics.

9

Published as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

## 7 ETHICS AND REPRODUCIBILITY

Our paper focuses on fundamental algorithmic advances to improve the generative modeling performance of SGMs. As such, the proposed CLD does not imply immediate ethical concerns. However, we validate CLD on image synthesis benchmarks. Generative modeling of images has promising applications, for example for digital content creation and artistic expression (Bailey, 2020), but can also be used for nefarious purposes (Vaccari & Chadwick, 2020; Mirsky & Lee, 2021; Nguyen et al., 2021). It is worth mentioning that compared to generative adversarial networks (Goodfellow et al., 2014), a very popular class of generative models, SGMs have the promise to model the data more faithfully, without dropping modes and introducing problematic biases. Generally, the ethical impact of our work depends on its application domain and the task at hand.

To aid reproducibility of the results and methods presented in our paper, we made source code to reproduce the main results of the paper publicly available, including detailed instructions; see our project page https://nv-tlabs.github.io/CLD-SGM and the code repository https: //github.com/nv-tlabs/CLD-SGM. Furthermore, all training details and hyperparameters are already in detail described in the Appendix, in particular in App. E.

coarse__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.label: Coarse (qwen3-235b-a22b-2507)
coarse__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.overall_feedback:
The paper presents a well-motivated and technically sound extension of score-based generative modeling by introducing critically-damped Langevin diffusion (CLD). It makes a compelling case for improved sampling efficiency and denoising simplicity through principled use of Hamiltonian dynamics and conditional velocity scores. The integration of ideas from statistical mechanics is elegant, and the empirical results on CIFAR-10 and CelebA-HQ are strong for the model size. The derivation of the hybrid score matching objective and the SSCS sampler are notable contributions.

Overview issues:

- Lack of ablation for critical damping assumption: The paper asserts that critical damping (Γ² = 4M) yields optimal convergence but provides no ablation comparing underdamped or overdamped variants under identical conditions. While Fig. 2 shows smoother score functions, it does not isolate the effect of critical damping from other aspects of CLD. Without direct comparison to non-critically-damped versions of the same model, the claimed benefits of 'critical' damping remain unverified. This is a central claim of the paper, yet the ablation studies in Section 5.3 only vary M⁻¹ while keeping Γ² = 4M, thus not testing the damping regime itself. A proper ablation would require fixing M and varying Γ independently to compare under-, over-, and critically-damped dynamics.

- Insufficient comparison to modern high-order solvers: The paper claims SSCS outperforms Euler–Maruyama and compares favorably to GGF, but omits comparison to more advanced fixed-step or adaptive high-order integrators designed for second-order SDEs, such as stochastic Runge–Kutta methods or geometric integrators. Given that CLD is inspired by molecular dynamics, where such solvers are standard, the evaluation of SSCS should include baselines like RATTLE or other splitting methods. The current comparison to EM is too weak to support the claim of a superior solver, especially since EM is known to perform poorly on Hamiltonian systems. This undermines the significance of the proposed sampling scheme.

- Overstated simplicity of the velocity score objective: The paper argues that learning ∇ᵥ log p(v|x) is easier than learning ∇ₓ log p(x) because the former is closer to a Gaussian. However, this overlooks that p(v|x) still depends on the full data distribution through conditioning, and the score must capture complex dependencies between x and v induced by the diffusion path. The empirical justification in Fig. 2 relies on a synthetic mixture-of-Gaussians example, which may not reflect the complexity in high-dimensional image spaces. No evidence is provided that the learned score function is actually simpler in practice—only Jacobian norms are shown, which conflate model smoothness with task difficulty. A more rigorous analysis of function complexity, such as effective dimensionality or spectral properties, would be needed to support this claim.

- Missing analysis of velocity initialization sensitivity: The model's performance is shown to depend on γ, the scaling of the initial velocity variance, with γ = 0.04 preferred for FID. However, no discussion is given as to why this value works best or how it interacts with the damping coefficient and time rescaling β. The choice appears empirical and unprincipled. Given that the entire framework hinges on the coupling between x and v, and that v₀ is unobserved, the sensitivity to this hyperparameter is a significant limitation. A more thorough investigation—such as how γ affects mixing time or score estimation bias—would strengthen the method's robustness claims.

- Unverified claim about hyperparameter transferability: The paper states that hyperparameters 'immediately translate to new tasks' based on using the same settings for CIFAR-10 and CelebA-HQ without tuning. However, the CelebA-HQ results lack quantitative evaluation (only qualitative samples are shown), so this claim cannot be assessed. Without FID, NLL, or other metrics on CelebA-HQ, it is unclear whether performance is comparable or whether the model generalizes well. This weakens the argument for broad applicability and ease of use, especially for high-resolution generation where diffusion dynamics may behave differently.

- No ablation of damping regime on sampling efficiency: The paper claims critical damping ensures optimal convergence, yet it never compares underdamped or overdamped versions of CLD in terms of sampling efficiency or sample quality under fixed compute budgets. The ablation in Table 4 varies only M⁻¹ while maintaining Γ² = 4M, so it does not test the actual damping regime. To substantiate the claim that critical damping is superior, the authors should fix M and vary Γ independently across underdamped (Γ² < 4M), critically damped (Γ² = 4M), and overdamped (Γ² > 4M) settings, then report FID and NFE for each. Without this, the central motivation for 'critically-damped' Langevin diffusion remains unsupported.

- Missing comparison to higher-order SDE solvers: The paper evaluates SSCS against Euler–Maruyama and adaptive GGF but omits comparison to established high-order integrators for second-order SDEs, such as stochastic Runge–Kutta or geometric splitting methods commonly used in molecular dynamics. Since the derivation of SSCS is inspired by such methods, it should be benchmarked against them to demonstrate its relative advantage. At minimum, the authors should compare to a fixed-step RATTLE or OBABO integrator under identical NFE budgets on CIFAR-10 sampling. Otherwise, the claim that SSCS is a superior tailored solver lacks sufficient context.

- No quantitative evaluation on high-resolution images: The paper claims CLD is suitable for high-resolution synthesis based on qualitative samples from CelebA-HQ-256, but provides no FID, IS, or NLL scores. This makes it impossible to assess whether the method scales effectively or maintains performance gains. To support the claim of broad applicability, the authors should report FID scores for the CelebA-HQ-256 model using 50k samples and standard evaluation protocols. Without quantitative results, the extension to high-resolution data remains anecdotal.

- Unvalidated claim of easier score learning in high dimensions: The paper argues that learning ∇ᵥ log p(v|x) is easier than ∇ₓ log p(x) because the conditional is closer to Gaussian, but this is only supported by Jacobian norms on a low-dimensional mixture-of-Gaussians example. In high-dimensional image spaces, no evidence is given that the score function is actually simpler to learn. The authors should compute and compare the effective rank or spectral decay of the Jacobians of the score networks on CIFAR-10 for CLD versus VPSDE. Without such analysis, the claim about reduced complexity remains speculative and untested in the actual regime of interest.

Recommendation: major revision

Revision targets:

- Include ablation studies comparing underdamped, critically-damped, and overdamped variants of CLD while holding other factors constant, with metrics on convergence speed and sample quality.

- Expand the sampling evaluation to include comparisons with modern high-order or geometric SDE solvers beyond Euler–Maruyama and GGF.

- Provide stronger empirical or theoretical justification for the claim that learning ∇ᵥ log p(v|x) is simpler than learning data scores, beyond Jacobian norms on a synthetic example.

- Add a principled analysis of the initial velocity variance hyperparameter γ, including its impact on training dynamics and generalization.

- Report quantitative results (e.g., FID, NFE) for the CelebA-HQ-256 experiment to support claims about hyperparameter transferability and high-resolution performance.

comments.title: Critical damping claim lacks internal derivation
comments.quote: For $\Gamma^{2}{=}4M$ (*critical damping*), an ideal balance is achieved and convergence to $p_{\text{EQ}}(\mathbf{u})$ occurs as fast as possible in a smooth manner without oscillations (also see discussion in App. A.1) *(McCall, 2010)*.
comments.explanation: The claim that critical damping ensures fastest convergence is supported only by a citation to McCall (2010), not by any derivation within the paper. The drift matrix $\bm{A} = \beta \begin{bmatrix} 0 & M^{-1} \\ -1 & -\Gamma M^{-1} \end{bmatrix} \otimes \bm{I}_d$ governs convergence, and its eigenvalues determine the rate. The characteristic equation $\lambda^2 + \beta \Gamma M^{-1} \lambda + \beta^2 = 0$ has discriminant $\beta^2 M^{-2}(\Gamma^2 - 4M)$, so real repeated eigenvalues occur at $\Gamma^2 = 4M$, minimizing the spectral abscissa. This standard result should be derived or referenced explicitly in the main text to justify the central design choice.

comments.title: Ablation does not test damping regime
comments.quote: Tab. 4 shows results for a CLD-SGM trained with different  $M^{-1}$  (also recall that  $M^{-1}$  and  $\Gamma$  are tied together via  $\Gamma^2 = 4M$ ; we are always in the critical-damping regime). Different mass values perform mostly similarly.
comments.explanation: The ablation varies $M^{-1}$ while enforcing $\Gamma^2 = 4M$, so the system remains critically damped in all cases. This does not test whether critical damping itself is superior to underdamped or overdamped dynamics. To support the claim that critical damping is optimal, the authors should fix $M$ and vary $\Gamma$ independently to compare regimes. Without such a study, the central motivation for 'critically-damped' Langevin diffusion remains experimentally unverified.

comments.title: Hyperparameter transferability claim lacks quantitative support
comments.quote: We expect our chosen hyperparameters to immediately translate to new tasks and models. In fact, we used the same  $M^{-1}$ ,  $\gamma$ , MS and HSM settings for CIFAR-10 and CelebA-HQ-256 experiments without fine-tuning.
comments.explanation: The claim of immediate hyperparameter transferability relies on qualitative samples from CelebA-HQ-256 without FID, IS, or NLL scores. Without quantitative evaluation, it is impossible to assess whether performance matches that on CIFAR-10 or whether the settings are truly task-agnostic. This weakens the argument for broad applicability. The claim should be revised to reflect that transferability is plausible but not yet quantitatively demonstrated.

comments.title: SSCS superiority over EM lacks advanced baselines
comments.quote: Our experiments show that CLD outperforms previous SGMs on image synthesis for similar-capacity models and sampling compute budgets, while our novel SSCS is superior to EM in CLD-based SGMs.
comments.explanation: The evaluation of SSCS compares only to Euler–Maruyama and adaptive GGF, omitting established high-order integrators for second-order SDEs such as stochastic Runge–Kutta or geometric splitting methods (e.g., RATTLE, OBABO). Given that CLD is inspired by molecular dynamics—where such solvers are standard—the omission undermines the claim of a superior tailored solver. A comparison to at least one fixed-step high-order method under matched NFE would strengthen the empirical contribution.

comments.title: Claim of easier score learning lacks high-dimensional validation
comments.quote: We empirically verify the reduced complexity of the score of  $p_t(\mathbf{v}_t|\mathbf{x}_t)$  in Fig. 2. We find that the score that needs to be learnt by the model is more similar to a score corresponding to a Normal distribution for CLD than for the VPSDE.
comments.explanation: The evidence for easier score learning relies on Jacobian norms and score similarity in a low-dimensional mixture-of-Gaussians example. In high-dimensional image spaces, no analysis—such as spectral decay, effective rank, or gradient variance—is provided to show that $\nabla_{\mathbf{v}_t} \log p_t(\mathbf{v}_t|\mathbf{x}_t)$ is simpler than $\nabla_{\mathbf{x}_t} \log p_t(\mathbf{x}_t)$. The claim that the task is easier remains speculative without such validation on the actual data domain.

comments.title: Velocity score gradient derivation ignores path dependence
comments.quote:
Without loss of generality,  $p_t(\mathbf{u}_t) = p_t(\mathbf{x}_t,\mathbf{v}_t) = p_t(\mathbf{v}_t|\mathbf{x}_t)p_t(\mathbf{x}_t)$ . Hence,

$$
\nabla_ {\mathbf {v} _ {t}} \log p _ {t} (\mathbf {u} _ {t}) = \nabla_ {\mathbf {v} _ {t}} \left[ \log p _ {t} \left(\mathbf {v} _ {t} \mid \mathbf {x} _ {t}\right) + \log p _ {t} \left(\mathbf {x} _ {t}\right) \right] = \nabla_ {\mathbf {v} _ {t}} \log p _ {t} \left(\mathbf {v} _ {t} \mid \mathbf {x} _ {t}\right) \tag {7}
$$
comments.explanation: The derivation assumes $\log p_t(\mathbf{x}_t)$ is independent of $\mathbf{v}_t$, but $\mathbf{x}_t = \mathbf{x}_0 + \int_0^t \mathbf{v}_s ds$, so $p_t(\mathbf{x}_t)$ depends functionally on the path $\{\mathbf{v}_s\}_{s\leq t}$. Thus, $\nabla_{\mathbf{v}_t} \log p_t(\mathbf{x}_t)$ is not necessarily zero. The simplification in (7) ignores this implicit dependence. The paper should justify under what conditions—such as linear response or local approximation—this term can be neglected.

comments.title: Conditional Gaussianity claim lacks justification
comments.quote: This is the case, because our velocity distribution is initialized from a simple Normal distribution, such that  $p_t(\mathbf{v}_t|\mathbf{x}_t)$  is closer to a Normal distribution for all  $t\geq 0$  (and for any  $\mathbf{x}_t$ ) than  $p_t(\mathbf{x}_t)$  itself.
comments.explanation: While $p_0(\mathbf{v}_0|\mathbf{x}_0)$ is Normal due to independence, the conditional $p_t(\mathbf{v}_t|\mathbf{x}_t)$ evolves under nonlinear filtering and can become highly non-Gaussian. The argument based on $t=0$ does not extend to $t>0$ without assumptions such as linearity or small noise. The paper should specify the conditions under which the conditional remains approximately Gaussian or provide a derivation (e.g., via linear response) to support the claim.

comments.title: Jacobian norm conflates model smoothness with task difficulty
comments.quote: We also measure the complexity of the neural networks that were learnt to model this score via the squared Frobenius norm of their Jacobians. We find that the CLD-based SGMs have significantly simpler and smoother neural networks than VPSDE-based SGMs for most  $t$ , in particular when leveraging a mixed score formulation (see next section).
comments.explanation: The Frobenius norm of the Jacobian reflects the smoothness of the learned network, not the intrinsic complexity of the target score function. A smaller norm could result from architectural choices or regularization rather than an easier learning task. The paper should clarify that this metric measures model behavior, not task difficulty, or supplement it with direct analysis of the target score (e.g., via sample-based estimation).

comments.title: NLL bound does not support mode coverage claim
comments.quote: our model reaches an NLL bound of 3.31, which is on par with recent works such as Nichol & Dhariwal (2021); Austin et al. (2021); Vahdat et al. (2021) and indicates that our model is not dropping modes.
comments.explanation: The claim that the model is not dropping modes based on an NLL upper bound is unjustified. A tight bound can coexist with mode dropping if probability mass is misallocated. The authors acknowledge the bound may be loose, and no explicit mode coverage analysis is provided. The inference should be removed unless supported by diversity metrics or coverage estimates.

comments.title: Inconsistent M⁻¹ and Γ relationship phrasing
comments.quote: CLD’s hyperparameters are chosen as $\beta{=}4$, $\Gamma{=}1$ (or equivalently $M^{-1}{=}4$) in all experiments.
comments.explanation: The phrasing "or equivalently" incorrectly suggests $M^{-1} = 4$ is a direct reparameterization of $\Gamma = 1$, when in fact it follows from $\Gamma^2 = 4M$. This could mislead readers into thinking the relationship holds independently of the critical damping assumption. The sentence should clarify that $M^{-1} = 4$ is derived under $\Gamma^2 = 4M$ with $\Gamma = 1$.

comments.title: Misleading comparison to VPSDE dynamics
comments.quote: Diffusions such as the VPSDE *(Song et al., 2021c)* correspond to overdamped Langevin dynamics with high friction coefficients $\Gamma$ (see App. A.2).
comments.explanation: VPSDE is a first-order Ornstein-Uhlenbeck process, not a Langevin equation, and does not involve a friction parameter $\Gamma$ or mass $M$. While both converge to $\mathcal{N}(0,\bm{I}_d)$, equating VPSDE to overdamped Langevin dynamics with high $\Gamma$ is misleading. The comparison should clarify that VPSDE lacks the second-order structure and Hamiltonian coupling of CLD.

comments.title: Ambiguous matrix grouping in drift definition
comments.quote:
We propose to augment the data $\mathbf{x}_{t}\in\mathbb{R}^{d}$ with auxiliary *velocity* variables $\mathbf{v}_{t}\in\mathbb{R}^{d}$ and utilize a diffusion process that is run in the joint $\mathbf{x}_{t}$-$\mathbf{v}_{t}$-space. With $\mathbf{u}_{t}=(\mathbf{x}_{t},\mathbf{v}_{t})^{\top}\in\mathbb{R}^{2d}$, we set

\[ \bm{f}(\mathbf{u}_{t},t)\coloneqq\begin{pmatrix}\left(\begin{matrix}0&\beta M^{-1}\\
-\beta&-\Gamma\beta M^{-1}\end{matrix}\right)\otimes\bm{I}_{d}\end{pmatrix}\,\mathbf{u}_{t}\,,\qquad\bm{G}(\mathbf{u}_{t},t)\coloneqq\begin{pmatrix}0&0\\
0&\sqrt{2\Gamma\beta}\end{pmatrix}\otimes\bm{I}_{d}, \] (4)
comments.explanation: The outer $\begin{pmatrix}\end{pmatrix}$ in $\bm{f}(\mathbf{u}_t,t)$ is redundant and creates parsing ambiguity. The expression $\left(\begin{smallmatrix}0 & \beta M^{-1} \\ -\beta & -\Gamma\beta M^{-1}\end{smallmatrix}\right) \otimes \bm{I}_d$ is already a $2d \times 2d$ matrix; wrapping it in another matrix environment adds unnecessary nesting. The notation should be simplified to avoid misinterpretation.

comments.title: Covariance matrix typo in $\bm{\Sigma}_t$
comments.quote: With $\bm{\Sigma}_{t}=\underbrace{\begin{pmatrix}\Sigma_{t}^{xx}&\Sigma_{t}^{xv}\cr\Sigma_{t}^{vv}&\Sigma_{t}^{vv}\end{pmatrix}}_{\text{“per-dimension” covariance matrix}}\otimes\bm{I}_{d},\quad\text{we have}\quad\ell_{t}\coloneqq\sqrt{\frac{\Sigma_{t}^{xx}}{\Sigma_{t}^{xx}\Sigma_{t}^{vv}-(\Sigma_{t}^{xv})^{2}}}.$
comments.explanation: The lower-left block of the matrix is labeled $\Sigma_t^{vv}$, but it should be $\Sigma_t^{vx}$. Since the covariance matrix is symmetric, $\Sigma_t^{vx} = \Sigma_t^{xv}$, but the current notation incorrectly duplicates $\Sigma_t^{vv}$. This undermines confidence in the derivation, even though the final expression for $\ell_t$ is correct.

coarse__glm-4.6.label: Coarse (glm-4.6)
coarse__glm-4.6.overall_feedback:
This paper introduces Critically-Damped Langevin Diffusion (CLD) for score-based generative modeling, proposing a novel diffusion process that augments data with velocity variables. The authors claim CLD provides smoother denoising, better sampling efficiency, and improved generation quality compared to standard diffusion models. The paper includes theoretical derivations, a new training objective (HSM), a tailored sampler (SSCS), and experiments showing competitive FID scores on CIFAR-10.

The paper presents an interesting connection between statistical mechanics and score-based generative modeling. The core idea of using critically-damped dynamics with velocity augmentation is novel and well-motivated. The experimental results on CIFAR-10 are strong, showing improved FID scores with fewer function evaluations. However, the paper has several concerning gaps: the theoretical justification for critical damping's optimality in the context of generative modeling is weak, the ablation studies are insufficient to validate key claims, and the scalability claims are not adequately supported.

Overview issues:

- Insufficient Theoretical Justification for Critical Damping: The paper claims critical damping (²=4M) provides "ideal balance" and "fastest possible convergence" but provides no rigorous analysis for why this holds in the context of generative modeling. The argument relies on classical mechanics intuition (Section 3), but the generative modeling objective differs fundamentally from physical equilibration. The paper needs either (a) a theoretical analysis showing critical damping minimizes some relevant objective (e.g., denoising complexity, KL divergence), or (b) empirical evidence comparing underdamped, critically-damped, and overdamped regimes with matched computational budgets. Without this, the critical damping choice appears arbitrary.

- Incomplete Ablation of Velocity Augmentation Benefits: The paper's central claim that velocity augmentation makes the learning task easier (Section 3.1) lacks proper validation. The authors show smoother neural networks qualitatively (Fig. 2) but don't isolate whether this improvement comes from (a) the velocity augmentation itself, (b) the critical damping condition, or (c) the new HSM objective. A minimal ablation should compare: standard VPSDE vs. CLD with same network capacity, CLD without velocity coupling (setting =0), and CLD with different damping regimes. Without these controls, the claimed benefits cannot be attributed specifically to the proposed mechanics.

- Questionable Scalability Claims: The paper claims CLD "can be readily used for high-resolution image synthesis" but provides only anecdotal evidence on CelebA-HQ-256 without hyperparameter tuning or quantitative metrics. The velocity augmentation doubles the state dimensionality, which should increase computational costs. The paper needs to report (a) memory usage and training time comparisons with baselines at higher resolutions, and (b) whether the performance advantages observed on CIFAR-10 persist at scale. Without this, the scalability claims remain unsubstantiated.

- Limited Baseline Comparisons: The experimental comparison (Tables 1-3) lacks recent strong baselines in diffusion acceleration. Notably absent are comparisons with (a) consistency models which achieve fast sampling with quality comparable to diffusion models, and (b) other second-order methods like HOLD (High-Order Langevin Dynamics) mentioned in the literature context. The paper should include these comparisons to properly position CLD's contributions relative to state-of-the-art fast sampling methods.

- Missing Analysis of Hyperparameter Sensitivity: While Section 5.3 shows limited sensitivity to M and , it doesn't analyze the interaction between these parameters and the critical damping condition. The paper fixes ²=4M but doesn't show what happens when this constraint is violated. A systematic study of how performance varies across the (M,) parameter space would strengthen the claim that CLD doesn't have "difficult-to-tune hyperparameters." Additionally, the choice of =0.04 for initial velocity variance lacks justification.

- Missing worked example of CLD dynamics: The paper introduces CLD as a novel diffusion process but never computes its behavior for a concrete, analytically tractable case. Readers would benefit from seeing the CLD equations solved for a simple 1D or 2D Gaussian distribution, where the forward and reverse processes can be derived analytically. This would demonstrate how the velocity coupling and critical damping manifest in practice, and provide a sanity check that the theoretical machinery works. For instance, compute the perturbation kernel p_t(u_t|u_0) for a 1D Gaussian and verify that the critical damping condition (Γ²=4M) indeed yields the fastest convergence to the equilibrium distribution compared to underdamped/overdamped cases.

- No demonstration that the result has bite: The paper claims CLD provides an easier learning target but lacks a concrete example showing this advantage. A minimal demonstration would take a simple synthetic distribution (e.g., mixture of Gaussians) and compare the learned score functions for VPSDE vs. CLD. Show quantitatively that the CLD score ∇_v_t log p_t(v_t|x_t) is closer to the analytical score than ∇_x_t log p_t(x_t) for VPSDE. This would directly validate the central claim that velocity augmentation simplifies the learning problem. Without such a demonstration, the claim remains speculative.

- Missing inference discussion for velocity variables: The paper derives the HSM objective but never discusses practical inference challenges when sampling velocity variables during training. Since velocities are never observed, the paper should address: (a) how the choice of initial velocity variance γ affects training stability, (b) whether the conditional distribution p_t(v_t|x_t) remains well-conditioned throughout training, and (c) potential issues with velocity initialization in practice. A brief analysis or empirical study showing that inference over velocities is stable across different γ values would strengthen the method's practical applicability.

- Incomplete comparison to existing thermostats: The paper positions CLD as inspired by Langevin thermostats but never formally compares it to other thermostatting methods mentioned (Nosé-Hoover, Bussi-Parrinello). Since the paper claims CLD provides new insights into SGMs through statistical mechanics connections, it should at least show how alternative thermostats would modify the SDE formulation. Even a brief analytical comparison showing why critical damping is preferable for generative modeling would substantiate the choice of CLD over other established methods from the field.

Recommendation: major revision

Revision targets:

- Provide theoretical justification or empirical evidence for why critical damping is optimal for generative modeling

- Add comprehensive ablation studies isolating the contributions of velocity augmentation, critical damping, and HSM objective

- Include quantitative scalability analysis with training/computation costs and performance metrics at higher resolutions

- Compare against recent fast sampling baselines including consistency models and HOLD

- Analyze hyperparameter sensitivity across the full (M,) parameter space including violations of critical damping

comments.title: Critical damping condition lacks mathematical justification
comments.quote: For $\Gamma^{2}{=}4M$ (*critical damping*), an ideal balance is achieved and convergence to $p_{\text{EQ}}(\mathbf{u})$ occurs as fast as possible in a smooth manner without oscillations (also see discussion in App. A.1) *(McCall, 2010)*.
comments.explanation: The claim that critical damping provides the "fastest possible convergence" lacks mathematical justification for the stochastic differential equation context. While critical damping minimizes settling time in deterministic second-order systems, the paper's SDE requires analysis of the spectral gap or KL divergence decay rate. The reference to classical mechanics (McCall, 2010) doesn't directly apply to stochastic diffusion. The paper should analyze the eigenvalues of the generator matrix to show that the real part of the dominant eigenvalue is maximized at $\Gamma^{2}=4M$, which would indeed indicate fastest exponential convergence.

comments.title: Error in Strang splitting convergence order
comments.quote: A finer discretization results in a smaller error (since $N{=}t/\delta t$, the error effectively scales as $\mathcal{O}(\delta t^{2})$ for fixed $t$).
comments.explanation: The error scaling claim is incorrect for SDEs. The Strang splitting formula achieves $\mathcal{O}(\delta t^{2})$ global error only for deterministic ODEs. For SDEs with non-commuting operators, the strong convergence order is $\mathcal{O}(\delta t^{3/2})$ due to the stochastic term $\sqrt{2\Gamma\beta}d\mathbf{w}_{t}$ in the $A_{O}$ component. The paper incorrectly applies the deterministic error bound to the stochastic case. Rewrite the claim as "the error effectively scales as $\mathcal{O}(\delta t^{3/2})$ for fixed $t$ in the strong sense".

comments.title: NLL upper bound formulation error
comments.quote: We also evaluate an upper bound on the negative log-likelihood (NLL): $-\log p(\mathbf{x}_{0})\leq{-}\mathbb{E}_{\mathbf{v}_{0}\sim p(\mathbf{v}_{0})}\log p_{\varepsilon}(\mathbf{x}_{0},\mathbf{v}_{0}){-}H$, where $H$ is the entropy of $p(\mathbf{v}_{0})$ and $\log p_{\varepsilon}(\mathbf{x}_{0},\mathbf{v}_{0})$ is an unbiased estimate of $\log p(\mathbf{x}_{0},\mathbf{v}_{0})$ from the probability flow ODE
comments.explanation: The NLL upper bound has a sign error. The bound should be: $-\log p(x_0) \leq -\mathbb{E}_{v_0\sim p(v_0)}[\log p_\varepsilon(x_0,v_0)] + H$, not minus H. Since $H = -\mathbb{E}[\log p(v_0)]$, adding H (a positive quantity) gives a looser upper bound. The current formulation with "-H" would make the bound tighter than it should be, potentially invalidating comparisons. The correct derivation follows from Jensen's inequality applied to $-\log \int p(x_0,v_0) dv_0$.

comments.title: Inconsistent FID comparison claim
comments.quote: The only models marginally outperforming CLD are LSGM *(Vahdat et al., 2021)* and NSCN++/VESDE with 2,000 step predictor-corrector (PC) sampling *(Song et al., 2021c)*.
comments.explanation: This claim is contradicted by Table 1, which shows NCSN++, VESDE (SDE) achieves FID=2.20, better than CLD's FID of 2.23/2.25. The statement should be corrected to accurately reflect that multiple models achieve comparable or better performance, or qualified to specify the exact comparison conditions.

comments.title: Missing justification for "easier task" claim
comments.quote: We derive a novel score matching objective for CLD and show that the model only needs to learn the score function of the conditional distribution of the velocity given data, an easier task than learning scores of the data directly.
comments.explanation: The paper claims learning $\nabla_{v_t} \log p_t(v_t|x_t)$ is "an easier task" but provides no justification. This is a crucial claim about computational benefits. The authors need either theoretical analysis showing the conditional score has better properties (e.g., lower variance, smoother gradients), or empirical evidence comparing learning difficulty on controlled examples. Without this, the claim remains unverified.

comments.title: Undefined analytic score term in objective
comments.quote: $\min_{\bm{\theta}}\mathbb{E}_{t\sim\mathcal{U}[0,T]}\mathbb{E}_{\mathbf{x}_{0}\sim p_{0}(\mathbf{x}_{0})}\mathbb{E}_{\bm{\epsilon}_{2d}\sim\mathcal{N}(\bm{\epsilon}_{2d};\mathbf{0}_{2d},\bm{I}_{2d})}\left[\lambda(t)\ell_{t}^{2}||\bm{\epsilon}_{d:2d}-\alpha_{\bm{\theta}}(\bm{\mu}_{t}(\mathbf{x}_{0})+\bm{L}_{t}\bm{\epsilon}_{2d},t)||_{2}^{2}\right],$ (9)
comments.explanation: Equation (9) trains the full $\alpha_{\bm{\theta}}$ to predict $\bm{\epsilon}_{d:2d}$, but the mixed parameterization defines $\alpha_{\bm{\theta}} = \ell_{t}^{-1}\mathbf{v}_{t}/\Sigma_{t}^{vv} + \alpha_{\bm{\theta}}^{\prime}$ where the first term is analytically known. Training the full term defeats the purpose of mixed parameterization. The objective should train only the residual $\alpha_{\bm{\theta}}^{\prime}$ by subtracting the analytic term from the target.

comments.title: Missing justification for quadratic sampling times
comments.quote: For methods without adaptive stepsize (EM and SSCS), we use evaluation times chosen according to a quadratic function, like previous work *(Song et al., 2021a; Kong and Ping, 2021; Watson et al., 2021)* (indicated by QS).
comments.explanation: The paper adopts quadratic sampling times without justification, merely citing previous work. This is problematic because CLD's dynamics differ fundamentally from standard diffusion processes. The quadratic schedule was designed for standard SDEs with different noise structures. For CLD's critically-damped dynamics, the optimal discretization may differ. The authors should provide theoretical justification or empirical demonstration that this choice works well for CLD.

comments.title: Inconsistent hyperparameter relationship claim
comments.quote: Intuitively, training with smaller  $M^{-1}$  means that noise flows from the velocity variables  $\mathbf{v}_t$  into the data  $\mathbf{x}_t$  more slowly, which necessitates a larger time rescaling  $\beta$ . We found that simply tying  $M^{-1}$  and  $\beta$  together via  $\beta = 8\sqrt{M}$  works well and did not further fine-tune.
comments.explanation: The text claims smaller $M^{-1}$ necessitates larger $\beta$, but then provides $\beta = 8\sqrt{M}$. This is contradictory because if $M^{-1}$ gets smaller, then $M$ gets larger, making $\sqrt{M}$ larger, which means $\beta$ gets larger. However, the intuitive explanation suggests an inverse relationship. Either the intuition is wrong, or the mathematical relationship needs correction to resolve the inconsistency.

comments.title: Unsubstantiated scalability claim
comments.quote: Our framework provides new insights into score-based denoising diffusion models and can be readily used for high-resolution image synthesis.
comments.explanation: The abstract claims scalability to high-resolution synthesis but provides no evidence. The velocity augmentation doubles state dimensionality, increasing computational costs. The authors need either theoretical analysis showing how CLD scales with resolution, or empirical results demonstrating successful application to high-resolution synthesis with reasonable costs. The CelebA-HQ-256 results are anecdotal without quantitative metrics.

comments.title: Ambiguous gradient notation for velocity components
comments.quote: Furthermore, $\nabla_{\mathbf{v}_{t}}\log p_{t}(\mathbf{u}_{t}|\mathbf{x}_{0})=-\ell_{t}\bm{\epsilon}_{d:2d}$, where $\bm{\epsilon}_{d:2d}$ denotes those $d$ components of $\bm{\epsilon}_{2d}$ that actually affect $\nabla_{\mathbf{v}_{t}}\log p_{t}(\mathbf{u}_{t}|\mathbf{x}_{0})$
comments.explanation: The notation $\bm{\epsilon}_{d:2d}$ is ambiguous. Since $\nabla_{\mathbf{v}_{t}}\log p_{t}$ is $d$-dimensional but $\bm{\epsilon}_{2d}$ is $2d$-dimensional, the relationship needs clarification. Define $\bm{\epsilon}_{d:2d}$ explicitly as the velocity components, e.g., $\bm{\epsilon}_{d:2d} = [\bm{\epsilon}_{d+1}, \ldots, \bm{\epsilon}_{2d}]^{\top}$, or use clearer notation like $\bm{\epsilon}_{v}$.

comments.title: Undefined weighting function λ(t)
comments.quote: \min  _ {\boldsymbol {\theta}} \mathbb {E} _ {t \sim \mathcal {U} (0, T)} \mathbb {E} _ {\mathbf {u} _ {t} \sim p _ {t} (\mathbf {u} _ {t})} \left[ \lambda (t) \| s _ {\boldsymbol {\theta}} (\mathbf {u} _ {t}, t) - \nabla_ {\mathbf {v} _ {t}} \log p _ {t} (\mathbf {u} _ {t}) \| _ {2} ^ {2} \right] \tag
comments.explanation: The weighting function $\lambda(t)$ appears in objective (6) without definition. In score matching literature, $\lambda(t)$ typically balances contributions across time steps, often chosen as $\lambda(t) = g(t)^2$ where $g(t)$ is the diffusion coefficient. The paper should define $\lambda(t)$ and explain its role, or reference where this choice is specified.

comments.title: Critical damping condition inconsistency
comments.quote: CLD’s hyperparameters are chosen as $\beta{=}4$, $\Gamma{=}1$ (or equivalently $M^{-1}{=}4$) in all experiments.
comments.explanation: The parenthetical "or equivalently $M^{-1}=4$" creates confusion. If $\Gamma=1$ and critical damping holds ($\Gamma^2=4M$), then $M=0.25$, making $M^{-1}=4$ correct. However, the phrasing suggests $M^{-1}=4$ is equivalent to $\Gamma=1$, which is misleading. The equivalence only holds under the critical damping constraint. Rewrite as: "CLD's hyperparameters are chosen as $\beta=4$, $\Gamma=1$, and $M=0.25$ (satisfying $\Gamma^2=4M$) in all experiments."

coarse__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview.label: Coarse (gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview)
coarse__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview.overall_feedback:
This paper proposes Critically-Damped Langevin Diffusion (CLD), an augmented-state diffusion process for score-based generative modeling. By introducing velocity variables and using a critically-damped Langevin dynamic, the authors aim to improve the smoothness and efficiency of the denoising process. The paper includes a novel score-matching objective (HSM) and a symmetric splitting integrator (SSCS) for sampling.

The paper makes a compelling technical contribution by applying concepts from statistical mechanics to improve diffusion-based generative models. The proposed framework is well-motivated and the empirical results on CIFAR-10 and CelebA-HQ are strong. The derivation of the hybrid score matching objective and the tailored SDE integrator are particularly notable strengths.

Overview issues:

- Unclear impact of the augmented state on computational overhead: The paper introduces an augmented state space (position and velocity) which effectively doubles the dimensionality of the input to the neural network. While the authors claim improved sampling quality, the computational cost of training and inference is not explicitly quantified in terms of wall-clock time or GPU memory usage compared to standard models with similar parameter counts. Section 5 mentions that the model uses 6 input channels instead of 3, but it does not detail the resulting increase in FLOPs or latency per NFE. To address this, the authors should provide a direct comparison of training time and inference latency per sample for a standard DDPM++ versus their CLD-SGM on the same hardware.

- Limited evidence for the 'easier score' claim: The core motivation for CLD is that learning the score of the conditional distribution p(v|x) is easier than learning the score of the diffused data p(x). Figure 2 attempts to visualize this, but the evidence is largely qualitative and based on a toy mixture of Normals. It is not clear whether this 'easier' task property holds for high-dimensional, complex image datasets like CelebA-HQ or ImageNet. The authors should supplement this with a quantitative metric of score-matching difficulty, such as the variance of the score estimation error or the training convergence rate, measured on the actual image benchmarks rather than just toy distributions.

- Lack of sensitivity analysis for hyperparameters M and Gamma: The paper sets the critical damping condition Gamma^2 = 4M and argues that this is an ideal balance. However, the ablation study in Table 4 tests only a few values of M and does not explore the performance degradation if the critical damping condition is violated (e.g., in the underdamped or overdamped regimes). Without demonstrating that the model is sensitive to deviations from this condition, the claim that critical damping is a necessary or optimal design choice remains speculative. Adding a plot showing FID versus the damping ratio (Gamma / sqrt(4M)) would provide much-needed evidence for the robustness and necessity of the proposed hyperparameter tuning.

- Theoretical justification of the SSCS integrator: The derivation of the Symmetric Splitting CLD Sampler (SSCS) in Section 3.3 and Appendix D relies on the assumption that the score model term S is small, allowing for a split where the Hamiltonian and Ornstein-Uhlenbeck parts are solved exactly. The paper does not provide a formal error analysis of this splitting approach compared to standard solvers when the score term is large, which occurs early in the reverse diffusion process. Since the score function is most complex when t is near 0, the assumption that S is small may be violated at critical points in the sampling trajectory. A more rigorous discussion or a small-scale experiment visualizing the integration error as a function of time t would significantly strengthen the case for SSCS.

- Empirical validation of the 'easier score' claim on high-dimensional data: The paper relies heavily on the claim that learning the conditional score of velocity is inherently easier than learning the data score, but this is only demonstrated on a low-dimensional mixture of Gaussians. To substantiate this claim for the image generation tasks that constitute the paper's primary contribution, the authors should compute the score estimation error (the L2 norm of the difference between the model output and the ground truth score) on a held-out set of CIFAR-10 images throughout the training process. Comparing this metric directly against a standard VPSDE model trained on the same architecture would provide the necessary evidence that the conditional score task is indeed more stable and easier to learn in high dimensions.

- Quantitative analysis of the damping regime sensitivity: The paper posits that critical damping (Gamma^2 = 4M) is an optimal design choice, but the provided ablations only test a few discrete values of M. To demonstrate the 'bite' of this specific design restriction, the authors should provide a plot of FID versus the damping ratio (Gamma / sqrt(4M)) for a range of values spanning the underdamped, critically-damped, and overdamped regimes. This would clarify whether the performance gain is a robust property of the critical damping condition or if the model is relatively insensitive to these parameters, which would impact the claim that CLD is a superior, well-motivated diffusion process.

- Formal assessment of the SSCS integrator's splitting error: The derivation of the Symmetric Splitting CLD Sampler (SSCS) assumes the score term S is small enough to justify the operator splitting. However, the score function is typically largest near t=0, where the reverse-time SDE is most sensitive. The paper lacks a quantitative comparison of the integration error (e.g., the drift/diffusion bias) between SSCS and a standard high-order SDE solver (like an adaptive-step Milstein method) at these critical time steps. A small-scale experiment measuring the cumulative discretization bias on a single image sample would demonstrate that the splitting approximation remains valid throughout the entire reverse-diffusion trajectory.

Recommendation: minor revision

Revision targets:

- Provide a detailed wall-clock time and memory usage comparison between CLD-SGM and a baseline model (e.g., standard DDPM++) on the same hardware.

- Expand the sensitivity analysis to include a plot of FID versus the damping ratio to empirically validate the critical damping assumption.

- Include a more rigorous discussion or numerical experiment regarding the integration error of the SSCS solver, particularly at small t where the score function is most complex.

comments.title: Unsubstantiated claim regarding task difficulty
comments.quote: We derive a novel score matching objective for CLD and show that the model only needs to learn the score function of the conditional distribution of the velocity given data, an easier task than learning scores of the data directly.
comments.explanation: The assertion that learning the conditional score of velocity is inherently easier than learning the data score is a central motivation, yet it is presented as a conclusion without rigorous justification. In high-dimensional image generation, the complexity of score matching is tied to the curvature and multi-modality of the target distribution, which does not necessarily simplify by conditioning on velocity. Please provide a formal argument or empirical evidence—such as a comparison of the training loss landscape or score estimation error—to substantiate this claim. If such evidence is unavailable, rephrase the statement as a hypothesis.

comments.title: Inconsistency in the scope of the score matching objective
comments.quote: We derive the corresponding score matching objective and show that for CLD the neural network is tasked with learning only the score of the conditional distribution of velocity given data
comments.explanation: The text claims the network learns only the conditional score of velocity. However, the CLD-SGM model must account for the position variable x_t to perform reverse-time SDE integration, and the neural network takes both x_t and v_t as input. Claiming it learns only the conditional score is misleading because the network must model the dependency on x_t to be useful for the reverse process. Please clarify that the network is conditioned on the data variable to estimate the velocity score.

comments.title: Inconsistency in Global Error Order Analysis
comments.quote: A finer discretization results in a smaller error (since $N{=}t/\delta t$, the error effectively scales as $\mathcal{O}(\delta t^{2})$ for fixed $t$). Hence, th
comments.explanation: The text correctly identifies that the Strang splitting formula is second-order, but the subsequent replacement of the exact propagator with an Euler approximation limits the global error to O(delta t). The claim that the error effectively scales as O(delta t^2) is misleading in the context of the final algorithm. Please clarify that while the splitting structure is second-order, the use of an Euler approximation for the score term limits the global convergence rate to first-order, consistent with standard EM methods.

comments.title: Inaccurate characterization of non-Gaussian diffusion literature
comments.quote: s. *Nachmani et al. (2021)* recently introduced non-Gaussian diffusion processes with different noise distributions. However, the noise is still injected directly into the data, and no improved sampling schemes or training objectives are introduced.
comments.explanation: The claim that Nachmani et al. (2021) introduced no improved sampling schemes or training objectives is incorrect. Their work, Non-Gaussian Denoising Diffusion Models, explicitly proposes a new training objective based on the variational lower bound for non-Gaussian noise and introduces specific sampling procedures tailored to these distributions. Please correct this misrepresentation of their contributions.

coarse__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.label: Coarse (qwen3.6-35b-a3b)
coarse__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.overall_feedback:
The paper introduces Critically-Damped Langevin Diffusion (CLD), an augmented state-space diffusion process for score-based generative models. The authors derive a hybrid score matching objective and a Strang-splitting sampler, reporting improved FID scores on CIFAR-10 under limited neural function evaluation budgets. While the physical motivation is interesting and the empirical results are competitive, the manuscript relies heavily on heuristic arguments from statistical mechanics without formal convergence guarantees or rigorous justification for the claimed simplification of the score matching target. Practical deployment is also limited by the absence of guidance mechanisms and incomplete computational comparisons.

The work offers a novel augmentation strategy for SGMs that successfully reduces the number of sampling steps required for high-quality synthesis. The derivation of the hybrid score matching objective and the SSCS sampler demonstrates solid engineering and a thoughtful connection to molecular dynamics. The CIFAR-10 experiments are well-conducted and the ablation studies provide reasonable hyperparameter sensitivity analysis. However, the theoretical claims regarding convergence speed and score complexity lack formal backing, and the practical utility is constrained by unaddressed conditional generation workflows.

Overview issues:

- Unsubstantiated claim regarding the simplicity of the conditional score target: Section 3.1 argues that learning $\nabla_{\mathbf{v}_t}\log p_t(\mathbf{v}_t|\mathbf{x}_t)$ is inherently easier than learning $\nabla_{\mathbf{x}_t}\log p_t(\mathbf{x}_t)$ because the velocity prior is Gaussian. This intuition overlooks the fact that the conditional distribution is a high-dimensional convolution of the data manifold with the diffusion kernel, meaning it inherits the geometric complexity of the data rather than simplifying it. The empirical validation in Fig. 2 relies on a toy mixture of Gaussians, which fails to capture the intricate structure of natural images. Without a rigorous information-theoretic bound or empirical Jacobian analysis on actual CIFAR-10 samples, the core motivation for CLD remains heuristic. The authors should either provide a formal complexity argument for the conditional score or replace the toy experiment with a dimensionality-reduction analysis on real data.

- Incomplete computational fairness and wall-clock reporting for SSCS: The paper compares SSCS against Euler-Maruyama and GGF primarily using Neural Function Evaluations (NFE) as the efficiency metric. While NFE is standard, SSCS introduces analytical propagation steps for the Hamiltonian and Ornstein-Uhlenbeck components that require matrix operations or solving linear systems at each step. The manuscript does not report wall-clock sampling times or FLOP counts, making it impossible to verify whether the reduced NFE actually translates to faster synthesis. Furthermore, comparing a fixed-step SSCS to adaptive solvers like GGF or RK45 ODE samplers on an NFE basis is misleading, as adaptive methods dynamically adjust step sizes based on local error estimates. The authors need to provide wall-clock timing experiments under identical hardware constraints and clarify whether SSCS's analytical steps introduce non-negligible overhead that could offset the NFE savings.

- Absence of convergence rate analysis for CLD dynamics: The introduction of CLD is heavily motivated by statistical mechanics, specifically the claim that critical damping ($\Gamma^2=4M$) yields the fastest non-oscillatory convergence to equilibrium. While this holds for linear harmonic oscillators, real data distributions are highly nonlinear and high-dimensional. The paper provides no theoretical analysis of the mixing time or convergence rate of the CLD SDE on general manifolds. The literature context explicitly notes that formal convergence guarantees for CLD in the SGM setting remain incomplete. Without a proof or empirical scaling analysis showing how the mixing time of CLD compares to overdamped or underdamped alternatives as dimensionality increases, the claim of universally accelerated convergence is unsupported. A formal derivation of the spectral gap or a dimensionality-scaling experiment would strengthen the theoretical foundation.

- Unaddressed challenges in conditional generation and guidance: The augmented state space $(\mathbf{x}_t, \mathbf{v}_t)$ fundamentally changes how classifier guidance and classifier-free guidance operate in score-based models. Standard guidance relies on the gradient of the log-likelihood with respect to the data variable $\mathbf{x}_t$.
